“One priority is to encourage households, as well as pension funds including the GPIF [Government Pension Investment Fund],  to increase their investment in Japanese financial assets."
This comment from Japan’s Finance Minister, Satsuki Katayama, contributed to a 9-basis-point decline in 10-year JGB yields and a 0.5% boost to the yen during Tokyo trading.
If this signals a rotation for Japan’s massive investment portfolios, and it's a big IF, one of the questions for global markets would be: which foreign holdings are likely to be trimmed first?
#economy #markets #japan #bonds #yen

tweets.text:
I have a bot running 24/7 buying and selling for 0 target profit just to waste IRS manpower and money.

I print and send the IRS all the documentation about my trades in paper form to stop them from automatizing the process with a computer program.

I mix blank pages, double print, ghost print or mark/write in them just in case they have a way to scan and use some sort of recognition software. That way they have to look at thousands upon thousands of pages one by one and look and random scribblings and drawn dicks while doing their job.

It's a whole pallet of papers with trades so I hire a guy to forklift that shit and send it just in the offchance that having to deal with a pallet is troublesome for them.

All the documents are numbered but I pack everything *completely out of order* (as in the lower numbers always happen to be at the bottom of the pallet) in case the order of the trades is important. If order is important they now have to deal lift boxes and have them laying around before they can even start checking the numbers.

My normal trades are also in those pages so they can't just ignore everything.

This costs me thousands of dollars every year. I make sure to send the invoices of how much all the processes cost me on top of the pallet so that whoever is in charge of it knows I'm wasting the equivalent of two months of his waging in asinine shit on top of his or her time.

It's nuanced, IMO: 

- $TSLA has the clearest path to close the gap between US/China in mass production. But to get this done, majority of components seem sourced from China? 

- $CCXI right now has a RoboFab for 10K+ units/year, and 75%+ of components sourced from the US. 

So they're probably the leader in terms of Made in America supply chains + commercialization. But can't get to China scale yet through just US supply chains. 

Figure is probably the other one with 12,000 target capacity, but can't comment much since their supply chain visibility isn't very public. I don't consider Hyundai/Boston Dynamics to be very American anymore so didn't include them.

tweets.text:
just picked up a Naruto vol. 1 first print with obi in BGS 9.2, high grade for this book.

heres my thesis. between the success of the One Piece live action on Netflix and the TCG boom, I think we're at the start of a nostalgia-driven revival wave across legacy anime IP. Naruto has a new TCG launching in 2027 and a live-action film in production. I believe manga is about to run the same playbook the comic market ran last decade when the Marvel movies hit.

look at the comp. Deadpool's first appearance is New Mutants #98 (1991) — published at the absolute peak of the comic speculation bubble, when print runs were enormous and every man and his dog was bagging and boarding day one. The result: a CGC census pushing 28,000+ copies.

and yet, despite that massive supply, a CGC 9.8 went from roughly $400 before the first Deadpool movie to around $2,200 at the 2021 peak,  a 5x on one of the most common key books in existence, purely on film-driven demand.

now flip to Naruto vol. 1. It released in 2000, when nobody on earth was treating manga as an investment these were read, creased, and thrown out. after nearly three years of Beckett grading manga, the census sits around 370 copies. I'd be surprised if it cracks 2,000 in the next few years.

it also gets even thinner, the Beckett census doesn't distinguish whether an obi is present. from scouring listings, intact obis are far rarer than people realize — my estimate is only ~25% of the graded pop retains the original obi. That's a hidden sub-census inside an already tiny population.

the reason i think having the obi will be a big deal in the future is by looking at rare book collecting. a first edition of The Great Gatsby without its dust jacket is a $5–10k book. With the original jacket intact? 300k+. The jacket alone carries 30–50x the value of the book beneath it — because it was fragile paper packaging that readers threw away, so the jacket is the true scarce object. Early in that market's history the jacket premium was a modest 2–3x. That's exactly where the obi premium sits in manga today. the obi has the same physics — designed to be discarded, torn on removal, routinely stripped by Japanese secondhand shops. I don't think today's 2x obi premium is the equilibrium. I think it's the floor.

Same demand catalyst as the comic run. A fraction of a fraction of the supply.

tweets.text:
Failure is a teacher.

If you have success, you did it right, so there is probably nothing to learn. But if you have failure, there is something to learn.

You have to make the most of your failures. Over time, I developed an instinctual reaction to failure. My instinct now is to view failure as a puzzle that will give me gems if I can solve it.

The puzzle is simply this: What would I do differently in the future so as not to have that result?

When you solve that puzzle, you write the lesson down. That is where principles come from.
#RayDalio #Principles #GrowthMindset #ContinuousLearning

tweets.text:
Hadn’t seen that review, will check it out.

At a high level, just as I think you are somewhat sympathetic to Ron Paul-style libertarianism, I’m fairly sympathetic to what you are trying to do in terms of reindustrialization, at least in terms of goals. And indeed I myself spent years building robotic DNA sequencing factories in the US, with some success.

I think our main difference of opinion is on what we respectively consider to be realistic. I don’t think it’s realistic to forecast the continued solvency of the US government, because the 2025 Financial Report estimates the true debt to be $193.6T — itself an enormous increase over the $175T of the 2024 report, and amounting to ~$570k for every man, woman, and child in the US. My view is that we are in the midst of inflationary default, a soft default, where living standards decline while prices soar. Keynesianism as the new communism.

As such, I think the only thing for it is to dust off the playbook of the 1600s-1700s, when startup societies could be peacefully formed in places like North America, and to update that playbook for the post-Internet world. There are a variety of ways to build such societies that don’t involve any physical conflict; for example, one can just set up in a pre-existing tech park or special economic zone, and go from there.

Anyway, I think that’s what it boils down to. You believe state capacity exists, that the state can be reformed, and your actions follow logically from there. I view the legacy state as bankrupt in an accelerating way, and therefore think we need to prepare for something more like the fall of the USSR in 1991 than the Rosie-the-Riveter reindustrialization of the 1940s.

I may well be wrong (indeed, in many ways I hope I am), but if I’m not then exit-to-build-anew-on-the-frontier is the same backup plan the English diaspora chose in the 1600s. And that worked.

Btw, here’s the numbers showing the $193.6T in debt as of 2025 (it’s probably $20T higher by now as these are a year old).
https://t.co/KDYDQcpUJr

tweets.text:
I often observe people making decisions if their odds of being right are greater than 50 percent. What they fail to see is how much better off they'd be if they raised their chances even more (you can almost always improve your odds of being right by doing things that will give you more information). The expected value gain from raising the probability of being right from 51 percent to 85 percent (i.e., by 34 percentage points) is seventeen times more than raising the odds of being right from 49 percent (which is probably wrong) to 51 percent (which is only a little more likely to be right). Think of the probability as a measure of how often you're likely to be wrong. Raising the probability of being right by 34 percentage points means that a third of your bets will switch from losses to wins. That's why it pays to stress-test your thinking, even when you're pretty sure you're right.
#principleoftheday

Global governments and central banks are increasing liquidity to manage debt at 8% a year. That's the hidden devaluation of fiat currency you don't see.

Add inflation on top and you've got an 11% hurdle rate. If your investments aren't hitting 11%, you are getting poorer.

the days of expensive AI is over

between 5.6 Sol, grok 4.5 and Meta muse 1.1 releases in the last 24 hours it’s pretty clear that cost-per-intelligence is just as important as intelligence itself

it no longer matters how smart your model is, the dollars you spend need to translate directly into useful 

personally pumped to see announcements like this, intelligence should be accessible to anyone and everyone
